original_abstract:
Integrating sex and gender considerations into biomedical research is essential for improving health outcomes and ensuring medical equity. This study investigates the impact of national funding policies and journal editorial standards on the inclusion of sex and gender analysis within scientific literature from 2000 to 2021. Utilizing the Dimensions AI database, we initially screened over 5.1 million articles and 4.4 million grants across diverse fields, including the clinical, biological, and health sciences. Following strict inclusion criteria that required the use of sex-disaggregated data or the analysis of gender-based health outcomes, we identified a core dataset of 324,639 articles and 21,250 grants for detailed analysis. 

Our findings demonstrate that proactive policy interventions—such as the NIH’s "Sex as a Biological Variable" (SABV) policy in the United States and Canada’s "Gender-Based Analysis Plus" (GBA+) framework—have significantly increased both the volume of targeted funding and the proportion of published research reflecting these characteristics. Interrupted time-series (ITS) analysis revealed immediate and long-term positive shifts in research outputs following policy implementation. Furthermore, we observed a global rise in sex/gender-reflective publications starting around 2017, coinciding with more rigorous editorial standards adopted by high-impact journals like Nature, Cell, and The Lancet. This trend was evident even in countries without formal national funding mandates, such as Japan and South Korea, highlighting the powerful role of editorial gatekeeping in shaping scientific practice. These results underscore the necessity of coordinated efforts between policymakers and publishers to sustain the momentum toward more inclusive and representative biomedical science.
humanized_abstract:
Despite growing recognition of the critical role of sex and gender in determining not only human health and disease but also how best to diagnose and treat medical conditions, the field as a whole remains largely without a mechanistic understanding of how biological and social factors combine to affect health outcomes for men and women differently. Moreover, biomedical research has not always considers sex and gender factors, potentially hampering progress in the field as a whole and leading to poorer health outcomes for women, who comprise half of the world’s population, and among other genders. Our study investigated the determinants of publishing studies that examine the role of sex and gender in the effects of interventions and of receiving research funding aimed at studying biological and gender-based health disparities. Using the Dimensions AI database, we started with more than 5.1 million articles and 4.4 million grants published between 2000 and 2021 across all fields of science, including clinical, biological and health sciences. After applying strict filtering criteria, corresponding to the use of sex-stratified data in the articles or gender health disparities research, we obtained a core dataset composed of 324,639 papers and 21,250 grants, enabling us to carry out a precise analysis of the effects of research funding and publication filters.

Sex and gender analysis of funding and scientific output increases over time within and across countries We assessed whether policy initiatives aimed at increasing the representation of sex and gender analysis in science have led to more research being conducted into the effects of sex and gender on health and disease. Prior to the implementation of the policy, total funding and scientific output related to the effects of sex and gender on health and disease were tracked in the US and Canada over the period 2006–2015. The NIH Sex as a Biological Variable policy was introduced in 2016 and, with the "Gender-Based Analysis Plus" framework, introduced by the government of Canada in 2017. Our assessment showed that the funding and output increased within the countries post policy intervention. Additionally, with the implementation of stricter editorial standards at high-impact journals in 2017, the global trend for Sex and gender analysis in biomedical science increased starting from about 2017. The trend is seen regardless of whether there is a national funding mandate (e.g. Japan and South Korea). Thus, successful policy-making involving governments, funding agencies and editorial boards in top journals may be key to increasing the volume of biomedical science that is sex and gender analytically enhanced.

document.input:
Integrating sex and gender considerations into biomedical research is essential for improving health outcomes and ensuring medical equity. These results underscore the necessity of coordinated efforts between policymakers and publishers to sustain the momentum toward more inclusive and representative biomedical science.
